# ruminant

[**roo**-m*uh*-n*uh*nt]

## noun

any even-toed, hoofed mammal of the suborder Ruminantia, consisting of the cloven-hoofed, cud-chewing quadrupeds, including cattle, sheep, goats, bison, buffalo, deer, antelopes, giraffes, and chevrotains.

## adjective

being or relating to animals that ruminate or chew the cud, typically those of the suborder Ruminantia.

Methane emissions from ruminant animals represent a quarter of all methane emissions in the region.

contemplative; meditative.

In a corner of the library, a ruminant scholar sat poring over a thick book.

ruminant

/ ˈruːmɪnənt /

## noun

any artiodactyl mammal of the suborder *Ruminantia* , the members of which chew the cud and have a stomach of four compartments, one of which is the rumen. The group includes deer, antelopes, cattle, sheep, and goats

any other animal that chews the cud, such as a camel

## adjective

of, relating to, or belonging to the suborder *Ruminantia*

(of members of this suborder and related animals, such as camels) chewing the cud; ruminating

meditating or contemplating in a slow quiet way

ruminant

Any of various even-toed hoofed mammals of the suborder Ruminantia. Ruminants usually have a stomach divided into four compartments (called the rumen, reticulum, omasum, and abomasum), and chew a cud consisting of regurgitated, partially digested food. Ruminants include cattle, sheep, goats, deer, giraffes, antelopes, and camels.

## Other Word Forms

## Word History and Origins

## Example Sentences

Specifically, cattle and other ruminants are a huge source of methane.

Rennet, an enzyme naturally present in the stomachs of ruminants, would prompt the milk to coagulate, separating into curds and whey, thus laying the groundwork for modern cheese production.

Defra said keepers of cattle, sheep, camelids, including llamas and alpacas, and other ruminants must remain vigilant and follow the restrictions applying to animal movements.

In the cauldrons, the researchers identified blood remains from ruminants, mainly sheep and goats.

The analyses allowed researchers to distinguish residual fats derived from milk, ruminant and non-ruminant animals, as well as of marine or plant origin.

# Ruminants

Ruminants are a diverse group of herbivorous mammals known for their distinctive digestive process, which involves a specialized stomach structure composed of multiple chambers. When ruminants consume food, it first enters the rumen, the first chamber, where it undergoes initial digestion. The food is then regurgitated as cud, chewed again, and swallowed into other stomach compartments for further digestion. This complex system allows ruminants to efficiently extract nutrients from fibrous plant materials, such as grass, leaves, and twigs.

There are over two hundred species of ruminants, including domesticated animals like cows, sheep, and goats, as well as wild species such as deer and antelope. Ruminants are crucial in various ecosystems, serving as both herbivores that help control plant growth and as prey for carnivores. They have adaptations that allow them to thrive in diverse environments, from the dry regions inhabited by Bactrian camels to the mountainous terrains where chamois goats reside. Ruminants play a significant role in agriculture and food systems, contributing meat, milk, wool, and other resources to human populations worldwide.

Ruminants are a diverse group of herbivorous mammals known for their distinctive digestive process, which involves a specialized stomach structure composed of multiple chambers. When ruminants consume food, it first enters the rumen, the first chamber, where it undergoes initial digestion. The food is then regurgitated as cud, chewed again, and swallowed into other stomach compartments for further digestion. This complex system allows ruminants to efficiently extract nutrients from fibrous plant materials, such as grass, leaves, and twigs.

There are over two hundred species of ruminants, including domesticated animals like cows, sheep, and goats, as well as wild species such as deer and antelope. Ruminants are crucial in various ecosystems, serving as both herbivores that help control plant growth and as prey for carnivores. They have adaptations that allow them to thrive in diverse environments, from the dry regions inhabited by Bactrian camels to the mountainous terrains where chamois goats reside. Ruminants play a significant role in agriculture and food systems, contributing meat, milk, wool, and other resources to human populations worldwide.

## Subject Terms

# Ruminants

Ruminants are herbivorous animals that store their food in the first chamber of the stomach, called the rumen, when it is first swallowed, then after some digestion has taken place, regurgitate it as “cud,” which is chewed again and reswallowed into another chamber of the stomach for further digestion. This maximizes the amount of nutrition the animal can derive from hard-to-digest plant food. Wild ruminants tend to eat very quickly, getting as much food mass into their rumens as possible, then retiring to places of safety where they can digest at their leisure. There are over two hundred species of ruminants, including [sheep](/research-starters/anatomy-and-physiology/sheep), cows, camels, pronghorns, deer, goats, and [antelope](/research-starters/zoology/antelope). They eat [lichens](/research-starters/science/lichens), grass, leaves, and twigs.

Ruminants belong to the suborder Ruminantia in the order Artiodactyla. Families include Tragulidae, which is the only group in the infraorder Tragulina. These animals include ten mouse-deer species in three orders. The other families, Giraffidae, Antilocapridae, Cervidae, Moschidae, and Bovidae, belong to the infraorder Pecora. Tylopods have three-chambered stomachs. Examples are camels and llamas. Pecora are sheep, goats, antelope, deer, and cattle. Most pecorids have horns or antlers. These true ruminants have four-chambered stomachs, whose compartments are called the rumen, reticulum, psalterium, and abomasum. The abomasum is most similar to the stomachs of nonruminant [mammals](/research-starters/zoology/mammals), while the other three compartments are developments peculiar to ruminants.

Ruminants chew or grind food between their lower molars and a hard pad in the gums of the upper jaw. The rumen collects partly chewed food when it is first swallowed. The food undergoes digestion in the rumen and passes into the reticulum. There, it is softened by further digestion into cud. Then, the reticulum returns the cud to the mouth for rechewing, which is mixed with more saliva. Swallowing the chewed cud next sends food to the third compartment, the psalterium, for more digestion. The psalterium empties into the abomasum, where food mixes with gastric juices, and digestion continues. Finally, the food enters the intestine, which absorbs nutrients that are then carried through the body by means of blood.

The stomachs of ruminants also contain numerous microorganisms. These help break down the cellulose in plants and protect the ruminant from the effects of any toxins used as defense mechanisms by the plants.

## Domesticated Ruminants

Cattle, sheep, goats, and [reindeer](/research-starters/zoology/reindeer) are all domesticated ruminants, although there are still wild species of each. The world population of these ruminants is slightly less than four billion. Ruminants are useful food sources for humans because they are large mammals, providing a lot of meat, as well as milk, wool, hide, and fuel. Because they eat plants, they are low on the food chain. Since 90 percent of the energy from any food source is lost in digestion, domesticated ruminants are relatively efficient transmitters of food energy from plants to humans. The large size of these ruminants, however, means that their metabolisms are relatively slow, and thus, they can afford the time it takes to digest grasses, leaves, and twigs through rumination, whereas smaller [herbivores](/research-starters/anatomy-and-physiology/herbivores) with higher metabolisms, such as [rodents](/research-starters/anatomy-and-physiology/rodents), must digest food more quickly and thus eat more nutrient-rich plant food, such as seeds.

## Some Wild Ruminant Species

Wild ruminants are important to food chains because they eat plants, preventing overgrowth. They also are eaten by [carnivores](/research-starters/zoology/carnivores) and [omnivores](/research-starters/anatomy-and-physiology/omnivores). Bactrian camels, with three-chambered tylopod stomachs, inhabit the steppes and mountains of the Gobi Desert. These two-humped camels are domesticated as food and draft animals in Afghanistan, Iran, and China. Bactrian camels subsist on a diet of grass, leaves, herbs, twigs, and other plant parts. Their humps contain stored fat. Given the extreme aridity of their native environment, ruminant digestion allows them to derive the maximum nutrition from scarce food supplies.

Chamois goats live in the mountains of Europe and southwestern Asia. Their diet consists of grass and lichens in the summer, while in winter, they eat pine needles and bark. Pronghorns live in the open plains and semideserts of the North American West. They are the only living Antilocapridae, relatives of antelope. These true ruminants eat herbs, sagebrush, and grasses in the summer and dig under the snow for grass and twigs in the winter. The large reindeer of northern Europe and Asia inhabit forests, grasslands, and mountains. Reindeer eat grass, moss, leaves, twigs, and lichens. Sable antelope live in southeastern African woodlands and grasslands, where they eat grass, shrub leaves, and twigs.

Note:
Ruminants include cattle, sheep, deer, goats, giraffes, camels, and llamas, although camels, llamas, and other [camelids](/dictionary/camelid) are sometimes not considered true ruminants because they possess a 3-chambered rather than a 4-chambered stomach.

| Ruminants Temporal range: Early Eocene – present PreꞒ Ꞓ O S D C P T J K Pg N | **Ruminants** are herbivorous grazing "Grazing (behaviour)") or browsing "Browsing (herbivory)")artiodactyls belonging to the suborder**Ruminantia** that are able to acquire nutrients from plant-based food by fermenting it in a specialized stomach prior to digestion, principally through microbial actions. Ruminating mammals include cattle, all domesticated and wild bovines, goats, sheep, giraffes, deer, gazelles, and antelopes. From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Hoofed herbivorous grazing or browsing mammals

| Ruminants Temporal range: [Early Eocene](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Early_Eocene "Early Eocene") – [present](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Holocene "Holocene") [PreꞒ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Precambrian "Precambrian") [Ꞓ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cambrian "Cambrian") [O](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ordovician "Ordovician") [S](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") [D](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") [C](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carboniferous "Carboniferous") [P](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Permian "Permian") [T](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triassic "Triassic") [J](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jurassic "Jurassic") [K](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") [Pg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paleogene "Paleogene") [N](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neogene "Neogene") |
| --- |
| [![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Ruminantia.jpg/250px-Ruminantia.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Ruminantia.jpg) |
| [Scientific classification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Taxonomy_(biology) "Taxonomy (biology)")[![Image 5: Edit this classification](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/OOjs_UI_icon_edit-ltr.svg/20px-OOjs_UI_icon_edit-ltr.svg.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Template:Taxonomy/Ruminantia "Edit this classification") |
| Domain: | [Eukaryota](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eukaryote "Eukaryote") |
| Kingdom: | [Animalia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Animal "Animal") |
| Phylum: | [Chordata](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chordate "Chordate") |
| Class: | [Mammalia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") |
| Order: | [Artiodactyla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artiodactyl "Artiodactyl") |
| _Clade_: | [Cetruminantia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cetruminantia "Cetruminantia") |
| _Clade_: | Ruminantiamorpha Spaulding et al., 2009 |
| Suborder: | Ruminantia [Scopoli](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scopoli "Scopoli"), 1777 |
| Infraorders |
| * [Tragulina](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tragulina "Tragulina") (paraphyletic)[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-Clauss2014-1) * [Pecora](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pecora "Pecora") |

**Ruminants** are herbivorous [grazing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Grazing_(behaviour) "Grazing (behaviour)") or [browsing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Browsing_(herbivory) "Browsing (herbivory)")[artiodactyls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artiodactyl "Artiodactyl") belonging to the [suborder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Suborder "Suborder")**Ruminantia** that are able to acquire nutrients from plant-based food by [fermenting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enteric_fermentation "Enteric fermentation") it in a specialized [stomach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stomach "Stomach") prior to digestion, principally through microbial actions. The process, which takes place in the front part of the digestive system and therefore is called [foregut fermentation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foregut_fermentation "Foregut fermentation"), typically requires the fermented ingesta (known as [cud](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cud "Cud")) to be regurgitated and chewed again. The process of rechewing the cud to further break down plant matter and stimulate digestion is called **rumination**.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-3) The word "ruminant" comes from the Latin _ruminare_, which means "to chew over again".

The roughly 200 species of ruminants include both domestic and wild species.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-Fern%C3%A1ndez-2005-4) Ruminating mammals include [cattle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cattle "Cattle"), all domesticated and wild [bovines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bovine "Bovine"), [goats](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Goat "Goat"), [sheep](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sheep "Sheep"), [giraffes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Giraffe "Giraffe"), [deer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deer "Deer"), [gazelles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gazelle "Gazelle"), and [antelopes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antelope "Antelope").[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-Fowler,_M.E._2010-5) It has also been suggested that [notoungulates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Notoungulates "Notoungulates") also relied on rumination, as opposed to other [atlantogenatans](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atlantogenata "Atlantogenata") that rely on the more typical [hindgut fermentation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindgut_fermentation "Hindgut fermentation"), though this is not entirely certain.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-6)

Ruminants represent the most diverse group of living [ungulates](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ungulate "Ungulate").[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-7) The suborder Ruminantia includes six different families: [Tragulidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tragulidae "Tragulidae"), [Giraffidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Giraffidae "Giraffidae"), [Antilocapridae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Antilocapridae "Antilocapridae"), [Cervidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cervidae "Cervidae"), [Moschidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Musk_deer "Musk deer"), and [Bovidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bovidae "Bovidae").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-Fern%C3%A1ndez-2005-4)

Taxonomy and evolution
----------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ruminant&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Taxonomy and evolution")]

The first fossil ruminants appeared in the [Early Eocene](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Early_Eocene "Early Eocene") and were small, likely omnivorous, forest-dwellers.[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-DeMiguel-8) Artiodactyls with cranial appendages first occur in the early [Miocene](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Miocene "Miocene").[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-DeMiguel-8)

### Phylogeny

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Ruminant&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Phylogeny")]

**Ruminantia** is a [crown group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crown_group "Crown group") of ruminants within the [order](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Order_(biology) "Order (biology)")[Artiodactyla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artiodactyla "Artiodactyla"), [cladistically](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cladistically "Cladistically") defined by Spaulding et al. as "the least inclusive clade that includes _[Bos taurus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bos\_taurus "Bos taurus")_ (cow) and _[Tragulus napu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tragulus\_napu "Tragulus napu")_ (mouse deer)". **Ruminantiamorpha** is a higher-level [clade](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clade "Clade") of artiodactyls, cladistically defined by Spaulding et al. as "Ruminantia plus all extinct taxa more closely related to extant members of Ruminantia than to any other living species."[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-Spaulding2009-9) This is a [stem-based](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stem-based_taxon "Stem-based taxon") definition for Ruminantiamorpha, and is more inclusive than the [crown group](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crown_group "Crown group") Ruminantia. As a crown group, Ruminantia only includes the [last common ancestor](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Last_common_ancestor "Last common ancestor") of all [extant](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extant_taxon "Extant taxon") (living) ruminants and their descendants (living or [extinct](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extinct "Extinct")), whereas Ruminantiamorpha, as a stem group, also includes more [basal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basal_(phylogenetics) "Basal (phylogenetics)") extinct ruminant ancestors that are more closely related to living ruminants than to other members of Artiodactyla. When considering only living taxa ([neontology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neontology "Neontology")), this makes Ruminantiamorpha and Ruminantia [synonymous](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Synonym_(taxonomy) "Synonym (taxonomy)"), and only Ruminantia is used. Thus, Ruminantiamorpha is only used in the context of [paleontology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paleontology "Paleontology"). Accordingly, Spaulding grouped some genera of the [extinct](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extinct "Extinct") family [Anthracotheriidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthracotheriidae "Anthracotheriidae") within Ruminantiamorpha (but not in Ruminantia), but placed others within Ruminantiamorpha's sister clade, [Cetancodontamorpha](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cetancodontamorpha "Cetancodontamorpha").[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-Spaulding2009-9)

Ruminantia's placement within [Artiodactyla](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Artiodactyla "Artiodactyla") can be represented in the following [cladogram](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cladogram "Cladogram"):[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-10)[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-O'Leary2013-11)[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-Song2012-12)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-dos_Reis2012-13)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-Upham2019-14)

Within Ruminantia, the [Tragulidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tragulidae "Tragulidae") (mouse deer) are considered the most [basal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Basal_(phylogenetics) "Basal (phylogenetics)") family,[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ruminant#cite_note-Kulemzina2011-15) with the remaining ruminants classified as belonging to the [infraorder](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Order_(biology) "Order (biology)")[Pecora](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pecora "Pecora"). Until the beginning of the 21st century, it was understood that the family [Moschidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moschidae "Moschidae") (musk deer) was [sister](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sister_taxon "Sister taxon") to [Cervidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cervidae "Cervidae"). However, a 2003 [phylogenetic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phylogenetic "Phylogenetic") study by Alexandre Hassanin (of [National Museum of Natural History, France](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Museum_of_Natural_History,_France "National Museum of Natural History, France")) and colleagues, based on [mitochondrial](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitochondria "Mitochondria") and [nuclear](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nucleus_(biology) "Nucleus (biology)") analyses, revealed that [Moschidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moschidae "Moschidae") and [Bovidae](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bovidae "Bovidae") form a [clade](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Clade "Clade") sister to [Cervidae](https://en.wikipedia

enriched_points.expanded_content: Ruminants are herbivorous mammals within the suborder Ruminantia, characterized by a specialized four-chambered stomach that allows them to efficiently digest fibrous plant materials.
Ruminants, including cattle, sheep, and goats, are vital for agriculture, providing meat, milk, wool, and other resources essential for human nutrition and economic stability.
Ruminants play a crucial role in food systems by converting plant biomass into high-quality protein sources, supporting food security and livelihoods worldwide.
Ruminants significantly contribute to greenhouse gas emissions, particularly methane, accounting for approximately 25% of total methane emissions, impacting climate change efforts.
